They play their home games at [[Wheldon Road]] (currently known as the OneBore Stadium for sponsorship reasons) and compete in the [[Super League]], the top flight of the [[British rugby league system]]. Castleford have won the [[Challenge Cup]] on four occasions, most recently in [[1985–86 Challenge Cup#Final|1986]]. Although the club has never won a [[List of British rugby league champions|League Championship]], they have been runners up three times and won the [[League Leaders' Shield]] in [[2017 Castleford Tigers season|2017]]. The club has rivalries with neighbours [[Featherstone Rovers]], [[Leeds Rhinos]] and [[Wakefield Trinity]]. The club's traditional home colours are black and amber, with orange also being used in recent years. == History == === 1896–1926: Background and formation === [[File:Castleford Tigers display Castleford Forum Museum.jpg|thumb|170px|Castleford Tigers memorabilia at the Castleford Forum Museum]] [[Rugby league]] in the town of [[Castleford]] can be traced back to the late nineteenth century. The original [[Castleford RFC (1896)|Castleford RFC]] joined the [[Northern Rugby Football Union]] in the breakaway league's second year, the [[1896–97 Northern Rugby Football Union season|1896–97 season]], and remained in the semi-professional ranks for ten seasons until withdrawing at the end of [[1905–06 Northern Rugby Football Union season|1905–06]]. Little is known about this original club, which was not directly connected to the current Castleford Tigers RLFC.{{Cite book |last=Garbett |first=Ian |title=Castleford Rugby League Football Club: A Ninety Years Statistical & Pictorial Record 1926-2016 |last2=Garbett |first2=Roy |date=14 August 2017 |publisher=Scratching Shed Publishing |others=In memory of [[Len Garbett]] |isbn=978-0995586109 |language=en}} The present club was formed in 1912, although no records are available prior to the team entering the Yorkshire Senior Competition in 1922, where they participated as amateurs for four seasons. An application to become members of the [[Rugby Football League]] was rejected out of hand in 1923, with further bids voted down in 1924 and 1925. However, at the RFL annual meeting on 17 February 1926, Castleford were successful in their application and were admitted to the league. === 1926–1950s: Establishment in the league === Having been elected into the Rugby Football League, Castleford joined the competition for the [[1926–27 Northern Rugby Football League season|1926–27 season]].{{Cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000324/19260218/004/0002 |title=Castleford's promotion |newspaper=[[Hull Daily Mail]] |date=18 February 1926 |page=2 |issue=((12,597)) |url-access=subscription |via=[[British Newspaper Archive]]}} The club professionalised, holding its first directors' meeting on 7 July 1926. They agreed to purchase their current home ground on [[Wheldon Road]] and, after a year making improvements, moved there in 1927. The club soon started to make a mark on northern rugby, claiming their first major trophy when they topped the [[Rugby league county leagues|Yorkshire League]] in [[1932–33 Northern Rugby Football League season|1932]], followed by winning the [[1934–35 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]] in 1935 by defeating [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]] 11–8.{{Cite web |last=Hampton |first=Paul |date=15 June 2017 |title=1935 Wembley |url=https://archive.castigersheritage.com/?d=true&i=531 |access-date=9 November 2025 |website=Castleford Tigers Heritage Project}} In 1939, they made it to the Championship final, but lost a close fought game against [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]], 8–6. The [[Second World War]] meant the league was suspended soon after, and Castleford officially abstained from league competition until the 1944–45 season.{{cn|date=November 2025}} === 1960–1972: Success === {{unreferencedsect|date=June 2025}} Castleford finished fourth in the national league in the 1962/63 season. The following season they lost 7–5 to [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] in the Challenge Cup semi-final replay at [[Belle Vue (Wakefield)|Belle Vue]], [[Wakefield]] in front of a 28,700 crowd after drawing at [[Station Road, Swinton|Station Road]], [[Swinton, Greater Manchester|Swinton]] in the first meeting of the two clubs. Under the direction of coach [[George Clinton (rugby league)|George Clinton]], Castleford won many fans in the '60s by playing an open and free-flowing style of rugby, earning them the nicknames "Classy Cas" and "High Speed Cas", the latter playing off the slogan for [[British Gas]] in use at the time.{{cn|date=November 2025}} Castleford picked up where they left off when they were again beaten in the Championship finals in 1969, this time conceding defeat to arch-rivals Leeds. However, this loss seemed to spur the team on, and 1969 ([[Salford Red Devils|v. Salford]]) and 1970 ([[Wigan Warriors|v. Wigan]]) saw Castleford win the [[Challenge Cup]] for two consecutive years, with clubs legends [[Alan Hardisty]] and [[Keith Hepworth]] leading the team.{{cn|date=November 2025}} === 1973–1995: Consistency === [[File:CastlefordTigersLogo.png|thumb|First iteration of a tiger motif upon the club crest, succeeding the town coat of arms, and used with slight variations from 1991 to 2025.|160x160px]] [[John Sheridan (rugby league)|John Sheridan]] was appointed head coach in 1973 for a spell. Castleford finished a respectable ninth in a one-division table but Sheridan stepped down following criticism from fans. During the late 1970s, Castleford edged up the league and in 1985 they made it to the Premiership final, where they were beaten by [[Hull Kingston Rovers]], and to the Yorkshire Cup final defeat to Hull KR at the beginning of the following season. However, in the [[1985–86 Challenge Cup#Final|1986 Challenge Cup final]], Castleford defeated Hull Kingston Rovers in a 15–14 triumph at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], with [[Bob Beardmore]] awarded the [[Lance Todd Trophy]] for man-of-the-match. They finished consistently high over the next few years, and finished in the top four clubs in the Championship for four years during 1990–1995.{{cn|date=November 2025}} [[Darryl van der Velde]] took Castleford to the [[1991–92 Challenge Cup#Final|Challenge Cup final]] at Wembley in 1992 where they were defeated by [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]. A year later, Darryl van der Velde left to become chief executive of the [[South Queensland Crushers]], and was succeeded by his assistant [[John Joyner]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} Through the Darryl van der Velde and early Joyner years, Castleford were lauded for their style and were labelled 'Classy Cas'{{Citation needed|date=February 2026}} . This playing style was to come to fruition in 1994, as they defeated a Wigan team to take the [[League Cup (rugby league)|Regal Trophy]] 33–2,{{Cite web |last=Hadfield |first=Dave |date=24 January 1994 |title=Rugby League / Regal Trophy: Wigan unrest highlighted by Trophy defeat: Headingley witnesses extraordinary turn of events masterminded by Ketteridge |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-regal-trophy-wigan-unrest-highlighted-by-trophy-defeat-headingley-witnesses-extraordinary-turn-of-events-masterminded-by-ketteridge-1402264.html |access-date=19 October 2025 |website=The Independent}} they were also semi-finalists in the [[Challenge Cup]] and were also defeated in the Premiership final. That season, John Joyner was named Coach of the Year by the RFL.{{Citation needed|date=February 2026}} [[St John Ellis]] scored a then-club-record 40 tries over the 1993–94 season.{{Cite web |last=Hadfield |first=Dave |date=4 January 2006 |title=St John Ellis |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/obituaries/st-john-ellis-6112704.html |access-date=19 October 2025 |website=The Independent}} === 1996–2004: Super League era === When a [[Super League]] was suggested, Castleford resisted a merger with [[Wakefield Trinity]] and [[Featherstone Rovers]], and became a founder member of the Super League in 1996.{{Cite web |last=Beattie |first=Geoffrey |date=16 April 1995 |title='If you're in some boardroom in Australia it might seem a good idea. But not around here' |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/if-you-re-in-some-boardroom-in-australia-it-might-seem-a-good-idea-but-not-around-here-1615851.html |access-date=13 November 2025 |website=The Independent}} The team performed weakly at the start of the season causing the resignation of coach [[John Joyner]]. The team avoided relegation by a whisker in 1997 following the appointment of [[Stuart Raper]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} The next season, they managed to frustrate the bleak predictions of pundits to move up the league, finishing sixth at the end of the season, after putting in some good performances and pleasing their fans with a sprinkling of victories.{{cn|date=November 2025}} In 1999, they continued on this upward trajectory, finishing fifth, as well as making the semi-finals of both the [[Challenge Cup]] and the Grand Final play-offs. This became one of the most famous seasons in the clubs recent past, with fans still remembering it with reverence. The team included many home grown players, such as [[Danny Orr]], and boasted that years [[Man of Steel Award|Man of Steel]] winner [[Adrian Vowles]].{{Cite web |last=Bazeley |first=Marc |date=23 August 2020 |title=Adrian Vowles: Life and leadership with Castleford Tigers icon and former Man of Steel |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/12055008/adrian-vowles-life-and-leadership-with-castleford-tigers-icon-and-former-man-of-steel |access-date=13 November 2025 |website=Sky Sports}} In 2000, the rise seemed to stall, as they repeated their fifth-place ranking and made the play-offs for a consecutive season. Raper left Castleford midway through the 2001 campaign to take charge of [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] and his assistant [[Graham Steadman]] took over the reins as head coach.{{cn|date=November 2025}} Castleford made the semi-final of the Challenge Cup in 2002, however the team was to fall down the table over the years to come. [[Gary Mercer]] guided Castleford to five wins in their last 10 games after replacing Steadman in 2004 but it was too little to save them from the drop as Castleford were relegated for the first time in the club's history.{{Cite web |date=18 September 2004 |title=Castleford suffer drop |url=https://newsimg.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/3666620.stm |access-date=13 November 2025 |website=BBC Sport}} Since the introduction of two divisions for the [[1973–74 Northern Rugby Football League season]], Castleford had spent 32 years in the top flight of British rugby league. Gary Mercer left the club following their relegation from Super League.{{cn|date=November 2025}} === 2005–2007: Life in the second tier of Rugby League === [[Dave Woods (rugby league coach)|Dave Woods]] was appointed head coach and Castleford finished second in the [[RFL Championship|Co-operative Championship]] in 2005. They were promoted back to the [[Super League]] via play-offs following victory in the playoff final against [[Whitehaven R.L.F.C.|Whitehaven]], as well as competing in the [[Northern Rail Cup]] final, where they lost to [[Hull Kingston Rovers]].{{Cite web |last=Walker |first=Gareth |date=18 July 2005 |title=Stand-in Webster walks tall for Rovers |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2005/jul/18/rugbyleague.castleford |access-date=8 December 2025 |website=The Guardian}} [[Terry Matterson]] joined Castleford in November 2005 in replacement of Woods. Castleford were celebrated for playing a good brand of rugby league, however it was not good enough to stop them from contesting a relegation dog fight which was to culminate in a historic match at [[Wakefield Trinity]]'s [[Belle Vue (Wakefield)|Belle Vue]], dubbed 'The Battle of Belle Vue'. It was a fight to stay in the league, and when Castleford lost to Wakefield Trinity, it confirmed their relegation.{{Cite web |last=Wilson |first=Andy |date=18 September 2006 |title=Cas crash leaves Matterson to rue 'crazy' British relegation fixation |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2006/sep/18/rugbyleague.sport2 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=The Guardian}} Many Castleford fans do not accept this relegation and it became a grave point of contention with the governing body. Castleford were relegated from second bottom in front of the newly inducted French side, [[Catalans Dragons]], who had been given immunity from relegation that season, and behind Wigan who had been found guilty of a breach of the salary cap rules.{{cn|date=November 2025}} In 2007, Castleford again made a quick return to [[Super League]] as they finished top of the Championship with only one loss all season and defeated [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] 42–10 in the Co-operative Championship play-off final.{{Cite web |date=7 October 2007 |title=Castleford 42-10 Widnes |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7026302.stm |access-date=8 December 2025 |website=BBC Sport}}{{Cite news |last=Wilson |first=Andy |date=8 October 2007 |title=Brough leads Castleford upwards |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2007/oct/08/rugbyleague.sport |access-date=8 December 2025 |work=The Guardian}} Castleford finished bottom of Super League in 2008, but were not relegated due to the newly in place franchise rules.{{cn|date=November 2025}} === 2009–2012: Re-establishment in Super League === In 2009, Castleford saw a brief return to success by reaching the Grand Final play-offs for the first time since [[Super League VII]] and made the semi-final of the [[Challenge Cup]] in 2011 before being knocked out by [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in an 8–10 defeat after extra time. At the end of the season, standout halfback [[Rangi Chase]] was announced as the winner of the 2011 [[Man of Steel Awards|Man of Steel]] award.{{Cite web |date=3 October 2011 |title=Castleford stand-off Rangi Chase is 2011 Man of Steel |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/15160070 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=BBC Sport}} Terry Matterson stepped down at the end of the 2011 season to take up a coaching role in Australia and was replaced by former [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] coach [[Ian Millward]]. Millward was released by mutual consent on 9 April 2013 after a poor run of results with 1 win in 18 games and with the team at the bottom of the Super League table. [[Daryl Powell]] was appointed head coach in May 2013 taking over from assistant coach [[Danny Orr]], who had been in temporary charge of the club.{{cn|date=November 2025}} === 2013–2021: Daryl Powell era === Under the guidance of [[Daryl Powell]] and his assistants [[Danny Orr]] and [[Ryan Sheridan (rugby league)|Ryan Sheridan]], the Castleford Tigers once again started to see success on and off the field.{{Cite web |date=9 March 2015 |title=Castleford's coaching staff commit to club |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/castlefords-coaching-staff-commit-to-club |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=Love Rugby League}} The [[2014 Castleford Tigers season|2014 side]] were again lauded as 'Classy Cas' for their fast-paced and exciting style, built around home grown players such as captain [[Michael Shenton]], [[Daryl Clark]], and [[Andy Lynch (rugby league)|Andy Lynch]]. The team reached the [[2014 Challenge Cup final|Challenge Cup final]] in August 2014, losing to local rivals [[Leeds Rhinos]] 23–10 in front of a crowd of 77,914 at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/28884420|title=Challenge Cup final: Castleford Tigers 10–23 Leeds Rhinos|date=23 August 2014|work=BBC Sport|access-date=29 July 2017}} Castleford missed out on the [[League Leaders' Shield]] on the final day of the regular season, eventually finishing 4th after defeat to [[Catalans Dragons|Catalans]].{{Cite web |date=13 September 2014 |title=Super League: St Helens win Shield after Catalan beat Castleford |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/catalans-vs-castleford/39949 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=Sky Sports}} In the play-offs, they were defeated 41–0 by [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in their qualifying final,{{Cite web |date=19 September 2014 |title=Super League play-offs: St Helens 41-0 Castleford Tigers |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/29220939 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=BBC Sport}} and beaten 14–30 by [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] in their preliminary semi-final to bring the season to a close.{{Cite web |date=25 September 2014 |title=Super League: Castleford Tigers 14-30 Warrington Wolves |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/29326859 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=BBC Sport}} At the end of season awards, Daryl Clark was named both [[Man of Steel Awards|Man of Steel]] and Young Player of the Year, and Powell was awarded Coach of the Year.{{Cite web |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=The Guardian}} On 29 September 2015, the club announced the death of chairman Jack Fulton.{{cite web |url=http://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/13995/10012159/tributes-for-castleford-chairman-jack-fulton-who-dies-aged-82 |title=Tributes for Castleford chairman Jack Fulton, who dies aged 83 |date=30 September 2015 |website=SkySports.com |publisher=[[Sky Sports]] |access-date=10 March 2016}} During the [[2016 Castleford Tigers season|2016 season]], winger [[Denny Solomona]] scored 42 Castleford Tigers season|2017]], the Tigers enjoyed a phenomenal 23 game regular season as they finished 10 points clear at the top of the table before the split, having won 20 and lost just 3 of their games. They went into the Super 8s having already secured a top four play-off spot and lifted the [[League Leaders' Shield]] in August, [[List of British rugby league league leaders|topping the table]] for the first time in their 91-year history.{{cite news |date=17 August 2017 |title=Castleford secure first league title after overpowering Wakefield |newspaper=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/aug/17/castleford-wakefield-super-8s-super-league-match-report}} In the play-off semi-final against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], Luke Gale – just days after undergoing an emergency [[appendectomy]] – kicked a drop goal in golden point extra time to send Cas to their first [[Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]] with a 23–22 victory. At [[Old Trafford]], Castleford were defeated 24–6 by Leeds Rhinos with a late consolation score from [[Alex Foster (rugby league)|Alex Foster]] their sole try.{{Cite web |last=Cartwright |first=Phil |date=7 October 2017 |title=Grand Final 2017: Castleford 6-24 Leeds Rhinos |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41536394 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=BBC Sport}} Gale was voted Man of Steel, while Powell picked up the Coach of the Year award for the second time.{{Cite web |date=3 October 2017 |title=Luke Gale: Castleford half-back wins 2017 Steve Prescott Man of Steel award |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41488323 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=BBC Sport}} The Tigers were also represented by six inclusions in the [[Super League Dream Team#2017|Super season|2018]] saw Castleford finish the regular season in 3rd position after an injury-hit year, including the loss of key man Luke Gale for several months with a fractured knee-cap.{{cite January 2026 |website=The Yorkshire Post}} In the [[2019 Castleford Tigers season|2019 season]], the club finished in 5th place on the Super League table. Castleford reached the second week of the play-offs, where they were defeated by eventual Grand Finalists [[Salford Red Devils]].{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/49814306|title=Super League: Salford Red Devils 22-0 Castleford Tigers|website=BBC Sport|date=26 September 2019|access-date=24 January 2026}} Castleford began the [[2020 Castleford Tigers season|2020 season]] strongly, however a slump in form following [[Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on rugby league|Super League's COVID-19 suspension]] led to a disappointing 8th-placed finish, their lowest since 2013.{{cite web|url=https://www.superleague.co.uk/article/1790/season-review-castleford-tigers/|title=Season Review: Castleford Tigers|website=Super League|accessdate=30 April 2021|date=28 December 2020}} Despite this, the inspired form of [[Paul McShane (rugby league)|Paul McShane]] saw him win the 2020 Man of Steel award.{{Cite web |date=23 November 2020 |title=Steve Prescott Man of Steel 2020: Paul McShane wins Super League award |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55049650 |access-date=24 January 2026 |website=BBC Sport}} In the [[2021 Challenge Cup]], Castleford twice prevailed in golden point extra time to advance to the semi-finals against [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]], whom they Sports}} === 2022–present: New era === At the conclusion of the 2021 season, [[Daryl Powell]] – by then Super League's longest serving head coach – departed Castleford, saying "For both me and the club, it is the right time."{{Cite web |last=Shaw |first=Matthew |date=19 March 2021 |title='It is the right time' - Daryl Powell speaks out about his decision to leave Castleford Tigers |url=https://www.examinerlive.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/breaking-daryl-powell-castleford-tigers-20211455 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=Yorkshire Live}}{{Cite web |date=19 March 2023 |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/12250409/super-league-daryl-powell-to-leave-castleford-tigers-at-end-of-2021-season |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=Sky Sports}} Alongside him, numerous long-serving and influential players moved on, including captain [[Michael Shenton]], [[Grant Millington]], and [[Oliver Holmes (rugby league)|Oliver Holmes]]. The new head coach was announced as [[Lee Radford]], ushering the club into a new era with a host of signings.{{Cite web |date=26 January 2022 |title=Season preview: Castleford Tigers |url=https://www.superleague.co.uk/article/2484/season-preview-castleford-tigers |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=Super League}}{{Cite web |last=Bower |first=Aaron |date=25 August 2021 |title=Castleford Tigers duo set to leave as rebuild under Lee Radford continues |url=https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/castleford-duo-leave-squad-rebuild-21400696 |access-date=26 December 2023 |website=Manchester Evening News}} Castleford started the [[2022 Castleford Tigers season|2022 season]] poorly before climbing into the playoff places by July. The final match of the year, against [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]], would see the winners advance to the playoffs – the Tigers conceded two late tries to lose 14–6, finishing their season in 7th place.{{cite web |date=3 September 2022 |title=Super League: Dramatic late Aidan Sezer try sends Leeds Rhinos into play-offs at expense of Castleford Tigers |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/castleford-tigers-unveil-new-badge-as-major-club-rebrand-revealed |access-date=19 October 2025 |website=Love Rugby League}} == Stadiums == === 1926–27: Sandy Desert === [[File:The Sandy Desert - geograph.org.uk - 649829.jpg|thumb|right|Sandy Desert in 2008]] In February 1926, Castleford RLFC were accepted into the [[Northern Rugby Football League]] from the [[1926–27 Northern Rugby Football League season|1926–27 season]]. Within a week of their successful application, the club announced that they had agreed to the purchase of [[Wheldon Road]] for their future home. While improvements were carried out, they played their first season as professionals at the Sandy Desert ground, located on the west side of Lock Lane, which had been their home as an amateur side.{{Cite web |last=Humphries |first=Allan |title=Early Days at the Jungle |url=https://archive.castigersheritage.com/?d=true&i=471 |access-date=28 December 2023 |website=Castleford Tigers Heritage Project}} The site is now used by amateur club [[Castleford Lock Lane]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} === 1927–present: Wheldon Road === {{Main|The Jungle (Wheldon Road)}} [[File:Wheldonroadmainstand.jpg|thumb|right|Wheldon Road]] [[Wheldon Road]] was built in 1926, originally intended to be the home of [[association football]] club [[Castleford Town F.C.|Castleford Town]]. In February 1926, within a week of Castleford RLFC's successful application to join the [[Northern Rugby Football League]], the club announced that they had agreed to buy the ground. They had negotiated an £800 loan from the RFL to finance the purchase. The club spent a year making improvements to spectator facilities, reseeding the playing area and replacing the perimeter fencing before finally moving in from the [[1927–28 Northern Rugby Football League season|1927–28 season]]. On 9 March 1935, the ground set its record attendance when 25,449 spectators watched Castleford play a third-round [[1934–35 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]] match against [[Hunslet R.L.F.C.|Hunslet]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} Castleford installed floodlights at Wheldon Road ahead of the [[1965–66 Northern Rugby Football League season|1965–66 season]], becoming the seventh rugby league club to do so.{{Cite web |last=Anderson |first=Tim |title=BBC2 Floodlit Competition |url=https://archive.castigersheritage.com/?d=true&i=472 |access-date=28 December 2023 |website=Castleford Tigers Heritage Project}} This enabled their participation in the inaugural [[BBC2 Floodlit Trophy]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} In the early 2000s, the stadium was renamed The Jungle as a result of sponsorship from online retailer Jungle.com. This moniker proved popular with supporters, with the club seeking to reinstate the name alongside subsequent naming rights holders.{{Cite web |date=29 August 2013 |title=Super League: Castleford confirm stadium to be called Mend-A-Hose Jungle |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/13995/8893643/super-league-castleford-confirm-stadium-to-be-called-mend-a-hose-jungle |access-date=28 December 2023 |website=Sky Sports}} [[File:WRE2.jpg|thumb]] [[File:Princess st.jpg|thumb]] [[File:Castigers.jpg|thumb]] === Proposed relocations === The Lateral Property group submitted a planning application for a proposed £135 million development and [[Five Towns Stadium]] in [[Glasshoughton]]. Outline planning permission was given to the development in 2015, with Reserved Matters applications to follow. In early 2018, Lateral Property Group announced that site had been renamed Axiom, working with partner Highgrove Plc,{{cite web|url=https://www.pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk/news/video-exclusive-new-name-for-five-towns-park-development-as-more-images-of-scheme-emerge-1-9019924|title=VIDEO EXCLUSIVE: New name for Five Towns Park development as more images of scheme emerge|website=www.pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk}} a scheme which would include food, a country park and an omni-retail experience. Numerous planning applications have been submitted through Wakefield MDC Planning Department to discharge conditions as well as amend the scheme slightly, with a Reserved Matters application for Phase 1 and 2a of the work approved. The schedule provided by the developers is that work on supporting roads and groundworks will begin in Summer/Autumn 2018 with the stadium to be completed in time for the 2021 season. In July 2018, an application was submitted to increase the capacity of the proposed ground from 10,000 to 10,245.{{cite web|url=https://planning.wakefield.gov.uk/online-applications/applicationDetails.do?keyVal=PCGM1TQQLLI00&activeTab=summary|title=Non-material amendment to vary the wording of condition 8 of approved Planning Application 16/01544/FUL |website=planning.wakefield.gov.uk}} == Kit sponsors and manufacturers == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |- ! |date=9 September 2025 |title=How Castleford Tigers' 2025 crowds compare to recent seasons |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/how-castleford-tigers-2025-average-crowd-compares-to-previous-years-with-worrying-dip-after-final-home-game |access-date=8 December 2025 |website=Love Rugby League}} The majority of the club's matchday support comes primarily from [[Castleford]] and the nearby towns of [[Normanton, West Yorkshire|Normanton]], [[Kippax, West Yorkshire|Kippax]], [[Knottingley]], [[Rothwell, West Yorkshire|Rothwell]], [[Garforth]], [[Selby]], [[Sherburn in Elmet]], and [[Cross Gates]].{{Citation needed|date=December 2025}} While Castleford spent the 2005 and 2007 rugby league seasons in [[RFL Championship|National League One]] due to relegation, the supporters maintained an average attendance above 5,000, enabling the club to retain a full-time squad.{{Cite news |last=Wilson |first=Andy |date=19 February 2005 |title=Big rush for Rail return to Rovers |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2005/feb/19/rugbyleague.castleford |access-date=8 December 2025 |work=The Guardian}} They broke attendance records in the second tier, including the [[Northern Rail Cup]] record of 9,400 for the [[2005 National League Cup#Final|2005 final]] against [[Hull Kingston Rovers]], and the Championship record attendance of 20,814 in the [[2007 National League One#Grand Final|2007 Grand Final]] against [[Widnes Vikings]].
